title: A Long History, but One That Must Be Remembered!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. The Tamil section is a source-checked reading copy derived from the OCR and does not alter the canonical source file. Source-page inspection confirmed `அ.தி.மு.க. தலைவி` where OCR had incorrectly produced `தி.மு.க. தலைவி`, and `1974ஆம் ஆண்டோடு காவிரி ஒப்பந்தம் முடிந்து விட்டது` where the year phrase was corrupted. The malformed duplicate subtitle and other unambiguous OCR word splits are also repaired. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: All the newspapers have reported that Chief Minister Jayalalithaa wrote to Prime Minister Narendra Modi on 13-6-2014 asking for the immediate constitution of the Cauvery Management Board. Yet only three days earlier, on 10-6-2014, this same Chief Minister issued a lengthy statement replying to me.

I had asked why, when the Karnataka Chief Minister invited representatives of every party in that state, heard their views, took them to Delhi and appealed to the Prime Minister over the Cauvery Management Board, representatives of all parties could not similarly be invited for discussions in Tamil Nadu. The Chief Minister issued her statement in response. She said the Prime Minister had listened sympathetically to her request and assured her that action would be taken; that he wished to help Tamil Nadu; that establishing the Cauvery Management Board formed part of the Cauvery Tribunal’s award; and that the Union government would require a little time to implement it. If, just three days later, she wrote demanding that the Board be constituted immediately, had she herself forgotten that her statement of the 10th asked that the Prime Minister be given time?

In her letter to the Prime Minister, Jayalalithaa unnecessarily dragged the DMK into a needless quarrel. As usual, she alleged that the former DMK government had failed to press the Union government to publish the Cauvery Tribunal’s final award in the Gazette. Whether the issue is Cauvery, Katchatheevu or Sri Lanka, it appears that Jayalalithaa cannot sleep without blaming the DMK! How many times must I answer her accusations against the DMK over Cauvery?

Jayalalithaa spoke in the Assembly as though her tireless efforts had secured the Cauvery Tribunal’s interim order. But it was when the DMK was in government that a letter was first sent to the Union government on 17-2-1970 demanding the establishment of a tribunal. It was under the DMK government that the Legislative Assembly passed a resolution on 8-7-1971 calling for such a tribunal.

It was again under DMK rule that an all-party leaders’ meeting was held on 27-7-1989 and unanimously resolved to ask the Union government to establish the tribunal. The leaders of every party except the AIADMK led by Jayalalithaa attended that meeting.

After V.P. Singh became Prime Minister on 2-12-1989, I wrote asking him to constitute the tribunal. He asked me to send a resolution from the Assembly. Accordingly, on 24-4-1990, the Assembly again resolved that a tribunal was necessary. As a result, the tribunal was established on 2-6-1990, under DMK rule, while I was Chief Minister.

On 28-7-1990, the DMK government petitioned the tribunal for an interim award. On 5-1-1991, the tribunal said it had no authority to grant one. On 10-1-1991, while the DMK was still in office, we went to the Supreme Court and asked it to decide whether the tribunal possessed that authority. On 26-4-1991, the Supreme Court ruled that it did. It was because of the Supreme Court judgment sought and obtained under DMK rule that the tribunal issued its interim award on 25-6-1991.

In the meantime, the DMK government had been dismissed on 31-1-1991 and Jayalalithaa came to power. Although the interim award was published in the Union Gazette during her rule, it produced no results. From 1991 to 1996, Jayalalithaa’s government made no effort to implement it.

After the DMK returned to office in 1996, I repeatedly asked the Prime Minister to finalise the implementation scheme and publish it in the Gazette so that delay in enforcing the tribunal’s order could be avoided. I wrote on 9-7-1997, 23-7-1997, 29-9-1997, 1-11-1997 and 6-11-1997, and met him in person on 27-7-1997 and 29-9-1997. On 10-11-1997, the Tamil Nadu government also petitioned the Supreme Court to direct the Union government to issue such an order. I wrote again on 28-3-1998, 6-4-1998 and 31-5-1998, and met the Prime Minister on 7-4-1998, seeking finalisation and gazette publication of the draft scheme.

On 21-7-1998, the Supreme Court observed: “It has been fifteen months since the Union government promised an amicable solution. No agreed scheme has yet been formed. This situation cannot be allowed to continue indefinitely.”

On 28-7-1998, Prime Minister Vajpayee invited me to a meeting arranged at the Prime Minister’s Office on 6-8-1998 to discuss the Cauvery issue. On 3-8-1998, I convened a meeting of all party leaders in Tamil Nadu to decide whether I should attend, and it resolved that I could. Jayalalithaa, however, issued a statement saying, “I should not go to that meeting.”

Only at the conclusion of the two-day meeting conducted by Prime Minister Vajpayee was an agreement reached on 7-8-1998, under DMK rule, to implement the interim award. It was decided that the 205 TMC of water specified in the interim order should be released and that a Cauvery Authority, headed by the Prime Minister and consisting of the Chief Ministers of the four Cauvery basin states, should be established.

Leaders including C. Subramaniam, G.K. Moopanar, Sankaraiah, R. Nallakannu, Abdul Samad, M.A. Latheef, Kumari Ananthan, Tindivanam Ramamurthy, R.M. Veerappan, G.A. Vadivelu, Ila. Ganesan, S. Thirunavukkarasu and Bishop Ezra Sargunam welcomed the agreement. So did the newspapers *The Hindu*, *The Indian Express*, *Dinamani*, *Viduthalai*, *Theekkathir*, *The Times of India* and *The Hindustan Times*, and the weeklies *Kalki*, *Kumudam*, *Saavi*, *Thuglak*, *Ananda Vikatan* and *Kungumam*. That is the history of what actually happened.
When every party leader and newspaper welcomed this historic agreement, did Jayalalithaa welcome it? No. Instead, she said that they rejected the decision and would not accept it.

The person who enabled that settlement was BJP Prime Minister Vajpayee. At the time, he was allied with the AIADMK. Even so, he invited me, the leader of the DMK, which was outside that alliance, and reached an amicable settlement. But do you know what the AIADMK leader, whose party was in the Union government, told reporters about the agreement?
“On the Cauvery issue, Vajpayee has fallen prey to Karunanidhi’s stratagem. We reject the agreement the two of them have made together,” she said.

The Cauvery Tribunal’s final award was delivered on 5-2-2007, during DMK rule. It was welcomed at the time in statements by M. Krishnasamy, then Tamil Nadu Congress president; Dravidar Kazhagam president and Tamilar Thalaivar K. Veeramani; VCK general secretary Thol. Thirumavalavan; CPI(M) state secretary N. Varadarajan; CPI Tamil Nadu secretary K. Pandian; Cauvery Delta Irrigation Farmers’ Association general secretary Renganathan; and K.S. Natarajan of the Farmers and Workers Party.

In her statement for the AIADMK, however, Jayalalithaa called the final award “an unacceptable judgment.” She said: “Karunanidhi has failed to obtain through the Cauvery Tribunal the full measure of justice due to Tamil Nadu. This final award is detrimental to Tamil Nadu. Accepting moral responsibility, the DMK government must resign.” As always, she demanded my resignation.
That very Jayalalithaa now claims that it was she who laboured to have the tribunal’s final award published in the Union Gazette, and says the DMK made no effort.

Even when the final award appeared, the DMK government did not wish to make a unilateral decision in the manner of Jayalalithaa. It convened all-party leaders’ meetings on 19-2-2007 and 15-4-2007 and decided only after consultation. Today, instead of honouring democratic convention by holding such a meeting, the Tamil Nadu Chief Minister acts as though whatever she thinks is the final decision.
The truth, therefore, is that Jayalalithaa accepted neither the tribunal’s interim award nor its final award. Did she at least accept the Cauvery River Authority?

The Cauvery River Authority was constituted and notified on 11-8-1998. Speaking in the Legislative Assembly on 2-4-2002, Chief Minister Jayalalithaa called it “an authority that does not function” and “a toothless authority,” and said, “We left the BJP alliance because we would not accept that authority.”

At a Cabinet meeting chaired by her on 21-6-2002, a decision was announced without carefully considering the consequences: “The Cauvery River Authority has no power and is incapable of effectively implementing the tribunal’s award. Tamil Nadu will therefore no longer participate in its meetings.”

It was Jayalalithaa who declared in July 2002 that the Cauvery Authority would be boycotted. The next day she said, “We shall see if we are invited.” As for me, even though I was not in office, I wrote to Prime Minister Vajpayee on 5-7-2002 asking him to convene the Authority immediately.
In his reply dated 10-7-2002, Prime Minister Vajpayee said he would look into the matter. The Cauvery River Authority was duly convened.

When Chief Minister Jayalalithaa attended the Authority meeting with the Prime Minister in Delhi on 27-8-2002, she did not engage in amicable discussion to find a way of obtaining water. Driven by her characteristic impatience and forgetting the urgent importance of the issue, she announced a walkout midway and left.

The Cauvery Authority met again on 29-11-2002. The Karnataka Chief Minister had arrived the previous day. Our Chief Minister Jayalalithaa said until the last minute that she would attend, and then cancelled the Delhi trip itself. Who suffered? The farmers of Tamil Nadu!

Meanwhile, Supreme Court judges told counsel for the Tamil Nadu government: “The letter written by Tamil Nadu Chief Minister Jayalalithaa to the Prime Minister stating that she had no confidence in the Cauvery River Authority headed by Prime Minister Vajpayee is highly objectionable. That defamatory letter must be withdrawn within four days.” Tamil Nadu government counsel K.K. Venugopal told the judges: “Tamil Nadu has complete confidence in the Prime Minister and in the Cauvery River Authority functioning under his leadership. Chief Minister Jayalalithaa will very shortly write again to Prime Minister Vajpayee expressing confidence in the Authority and withdrawing the earlier letter.”

Accordingly, in her letter to the Prime Minister on 16-11-2002, Jayalalithaa wrote that she personally held him in the highest regard and, forgetting everything she had previously said, that she also had confidence in the Cauvery Authority.
To summarise the many ways Jayalalithaa disregarded the welfare of delta farmers at every stage of the Cauvery issue:
When the tribunal delivered its interim award, it was Jayalalithaa who said, “We shall get water only if the Karnataka government opens the sluices of its own accord.”
When the Union government sought clarification on implementing the interim order, it was Jayalalithaa who replied only after a delay of nine months.
During her five years in office from 1991 to 1996, Jayalalithaa made no effort to implement the interim order.
In a petition submitted by the AIADMK to Prime Minister V.P. Singh, it was Jayalalithaa who wrote, “The Cauvery agreement expired with the year 1974.”
It was Jayalalithaa who cancelled her participation in the Cauvery Authority meeting on 29-11-2002. The list could go on.

Jayalalithaa imagines that the towns, the country and the world have forgotten this date-by-date history, and that she can say whatever she pleases. It is with that thought that she seeks to throw dust upon me. The DMK may have lost its chance of victory in the recently concluded parliamentary election. But whether Chief Minister Jayalalithaa issues a statement or writes to the Prime Minister, her thoughts keep circling around the DMK. From that alone, the world will understand who has achieved the real victory!
With affection,
M.K.
